article.title: The lost boys, thrown out of US sect so older men can marry more wives (2005)

summary: The Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (FLDS) has expelled hundreds of teenage boys, known as the 'Lost Boys,' to enable older men to marry multiple wives, a practice central to their belief system. This purge has led to significant social and legal turmoil, including asset freezes and criminal charges against FLDS leader Warren Jeffs, highlighting the sect's authoritarian control and human rights concerns.
keyPoints: FLDS requires men to marry at least three women to attain heaven, driving a gender imbalance.
Between 400 and 1,000 teenage boys have been expelled since 2002 to free up females for polygamous marriages.
Warren Jeffs, FLDS leader since 2002, has 40 wives and 56 children; charged with arranging an underage marriage.
Authorities froze FLDS assets in 2005 and issued an arrest warrant for Jeffs, who is believed to be hiding in Texas.
Expelled boys face social isolation, trauma, and rejection from families under sect orders.
The FLDS maintains strict control over members, limiting outside contact and enforcing harsh discipline.

rewrite.title: FLDS Expels Boys to Enable Polygamous Marriages
rewrite.body:
The Fundamentalist Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints (FLDS), a polygamous sect with approximately 10,000 members, has systematically expelled hundreds of teenage boys, known as the 'Lost Boys,' from its communities in Arizona and Utah. This practice, driven by the sect's belief that a man must marry at least three women to reach heaven, creates a gender imbalance that older men exploit by removing younger males to increase the pool of available females.

Since Warren Jeffs assumed leadership in 2002 following his father's death, he has enforced strict discipline, purging between 400 and 1,000 boys and young men deemed noncompliant or delinquent. Jeffs, aged 49, reportedly has 40 wives and 56 children. His authoritarian rule has sparked legal action: in 2005, a Utah judge froze FLDS assets, and Arizona authorities charged Jeffs with arranging a marriage between a 28-year-old man and a 16-year-old girl. Jeffs is currently believed to be hiding in an FLDS compound near El Dorado, Texas.

The expelled boys often face harsh consequences, including being abandoned on roadsides and ostracized by their families under sect directives, leading to significant psychological trauma. Organizations like Smiles for Diversity, founded by former FLDS members, work to support these youths scattered across the region. The FLDS’s insular nature and fear-based control complicate efforts by authorities to gather evidence or intervene.

This ongoing crisis exposes the intersection of religious extremism, child welfare, and legal challenges, raising questions about the protection of vulnerable individuals within closed religious communities and the limits of state intervention in religious practices.
rewrite.disclaimer: AI-assisted rewrite based on extracted text + discussion signals
